question: Which statement about configuring multi-tenancy on a Java job worker is **NOT** true?

options.text: If the client is unauthorised for one of the configured tenants, the worker still picks up jobs for the other authorised tenants.
`client.newWorker().tenantIds("myTenant", "myOtherTenant").open()` opens one worker that picks up jobs for both tenants.
Default tenants for every opened worker can be set with the env var `CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS` (or the equivalent system property).
A single-tenant worker is opened with `client.newWorker().tenantId("myTenant").open()`.

a.text: This is the false statement (and therefore the correct pick for a NOT question). The docs are explicit: the client must be authorised for ALL provided tenants; if it is not, the job worker will not work on any jobs at all — there is no silent subsetting to the authorised ones.

b.text: The `.tenantIds("myTenant", "myOtherTenant")` builder call really does open a worker for both tenants in a single subscription — this statement is true.

c.text: Setting `CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS` (or `camunda.client.worker.tenantIds`) does configure default tenants for every worker opened by that client — this statement is true.

d.text: Calling `.tenantId("myTenant")` opens a single-tenant worker exactly as the docs show — this statement is true.

explanation: Java job workers can be scoped to one tenant (`.tenantId`), to several (`.tenantIds`), or driven by client-wide defaults (`CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS` / `camunda.client.worker.tenantIds`). The critical authorisation rule is all-or-nothing: the client must be authorised for every configured tenant. If authorisation is missing for any one of them, the worker activates no jobs — not even for the tenants it is authorised for.
